petitioner_background: The petitioner is a journalist from Brazil specializing in automotive journalism with over 25 years of experience in the field. He has also worked as a business owner and executive in the communications industry, as an elected city councilor in municipal government in Brazil, and as a parliamentary assistant for the legislative assembly of the State of Sao Paulo. He sought classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under INA section 203(b)(1)(A) and intends to continue working as an automobile journalist in the United States.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence relating to lesser nationally or internationally recognized awards or prizes for excellence in the field, but the specific awards are not detailed in the decision because the petitioner did not contest the Director's negative determination on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined that the petitioner did not satisfy this criterion. Although the petitioner submitted a brief on appeal, he did not contest the Director's determination that he did not satisfy 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i). The AAO held that an issue not raised on appeal is waived, citing Matter of O-R-E- and Matter of R-A-M-.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted published materials from various Brazilian print and online publications, including A Vos do Pavo, Jornal do Interior, Jornal Votura, Cheiro do Mato, Comandonoticia.com, and ShopCar News. In response to the RFE, he focused on an article published in Falha de Sao Paulo about the upcoming release of the Chevrolet Vectra in the Brazilian market, in which the petitioner was credited for contributing photographs. On appeal, he requested reevaluation of two newspaper articles published in Jornal Votura in June 2012 regarding a project he undertook as director of a company to bring newspapers to local schools. He claimed Jornal Votura has wide reach in the state of Sao Paulo (with 44.04 million inhabitants), is part of a group with more than 39 years' experience in the market, is now known as Jornal Indaiatuba News, and boasts a social media following of over 40,000 individuals (with a 2024 screenshot showing 44,000 Facebook followers).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director noted that the submitted articles did not discuss the petitioner's work as a journalist but rather his ostrich farm, his work as a company director, and his work in local government. The record also lacked documentation regarding the publications' intended audience or data regarding relative circulation, readership or distribution. Regarding the Falha de Sao Paulo article, while the AAO found sufficient evidence that this publication qualifies as major media in Brazil, the article was about an automobile release and merely credited the petitioner for contributing photographs — it was not published material about the petitioner relating to his work in the field. Regarding the Jornal Votura articles, while they did relate to his work in journalism, the record lacked evidence that this newspaper qualifies as major media. The statements about its reach in Sao Paulo state and being part of a 39-year-old group were insufficient, and the social media following information was also insufficient to demonstrate major media status. The record lacked evidence regarding circulation and how it compares to other newspapers in Brazil.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner provided evidence that he served as a jury member for the annual Brazilian Automotive Press Association's ABIAUTO awards and for Brazil's Top Car TV awards. He emphasized that the ABIAUTO awards bring together more than 40 specialized journalists from throughout Brazil who participate in elections of the best cars in Brazil, divided into eight categories. The Top Car TV awards follow a similar process and serve a similar purpose. On appeal, he provided additional background on automotive journalism, arguing that automotive journalists must possess an intimate understanding of the automotive industry.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO agreed with the Director that the petitioner's judging activities do not satisfy the plain language of the criterion. The regulation requires evidence that the individual has judged the work of others in the same or an allied field of specialization for which classification is sought. The petitioner seeks classification based on his expertise in journalism (specifically automotive journalism), and his participation on juries selecting the 'best cars' in different vehicle categories does not involve judging the work of other individuals working in his field or an allied field. The juries evaluated features and performance of passenger vehicles, not the work or performance of journalists or individuals in the automotive industry. The AAO rejected the petitioner's analogy to a professor reviewing student papers, noting that while such activity would qualify, the petitioner had not presented evidence that he judged the work of students in journalism. The AAO noted the judging evidence could be considered in a final merits determination of national acclaim but did not satisfy the plain language of the criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner relied on his receipt of certain awards, publication of articles and reviews as an automotive journalist, publication of a book on the history of a municipality, career as an elected official, public service projects related to his government career, and participation in automotive industry events. In response to the RFE, he focused on his book about the history of the municipality, submitting: a statement from the book's publisher indicating 1,000 copies were printed in 2002; a declaration from the municipal secretary of education stating the book is available in the Municipal Library and used in schools; his own statement explaining his motivation and claiming the work has been used for over 20 years in the municipal education network reaching approximately 10,000 students. On appeal, he argued the book represents a substantial contribution to historical documentation, has sustained integration in the educational framework for over two decades, and invoked 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4) comparable evidence provision. He also emphasized he authored numerous original articles in widely circulated regional periodicals and contributed to Falha de Sao Paulo. He submitted recommendation letters, including from N-N- (fellow journalist praising his professional capacity and high-quality content for the car segment), P-L-D- (retired General Motors executive describing him as one of the most qualified automotive journalistic writers and photographers), and the founder of Carro magazine (stating he has remarkable knowledge and contributed to the history of automotive journalism in Brazil).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director granted that the petitioner's book is an original contribution significant to the municipality but concluded it was not of major significance in the field of journalism. The AAO agreed, noting the record does not demonstrate the book has been cited by other authors, provoked widespread commentary on its importance, or had influence or impact rising to the level of major significance. The fact that it is available at the municipal library and used in local schools is insufficient, and only 1,000 copies were printed (not widely distributed). The book was written to fulfill an undergraduate course requirement. The AAO rejected the comparable evidence argument because the petitioner did not articulate that 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(v) is not readily applicable to his occupation, and in fact claimed his body of work as an automotive journalist represents an original contribution. Regarding his automotive articles, the AAO found that simply demonstrating he authored articles and reviews reaching a significant regional audience does not demonstrate major significance. The recommendation letters offered enthusiastic praise but did not describe specific original contributions and their significance — they did not demonstrate how his publication of automobile reviews in mostly regional newspapers and magazines presents a contribution of major significance in automotive journalism. Such reviews are universally intended to provide consumer information while promoting manufacturers' latest models, regardless of author.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed he earned a high salary as a 'Parliamentary Assistant III' with the Legislative Assembly of the State of Sao Paulo, stating his monthly remuneration was more than double the average monthly salary for journalists in Brazil. He submitted: pay statements from November 2021 to January 2022; his Brazilian Individual Income Tax Return for 2021; 'Salary Floor' information for journalists published by the Union of Professional Journalists in the State of Sao Paolo; salaries for journalists in Brazil from Glassdoor.com; job duties and requirements for Parliamentary Assistant positions; and an excerpt from a February 2019 resolution from the Board of the Legislative Assembly regarding staff appointment procedures. Pay statements showed total monthly payments ranging from R$ 7,025 to R$ 8,786, including a base salary of R$ 3,075.71, a legislative bonus of over R$ 1,800, a representation bonus of over R$ 1,100, and varying amounts of food aid and health aid.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO disagreed with the Director that the petitioner did not provide an appropriate basis for salary comparison, noting the Parliamentary Assistant III position performs activities typical of the journalist profession and requires professional registration as a journalist. The petitioner's position was referred to internally as 'press officer' based on his government-issued credential, and the salary floor data from the Union of Professional Journalists included salaries for those employed by press offices. However, the comparative salary data did not demonstrate the petitioner earned a high salary. The Union's salary floor indicated standard salaries of R$ 4,057 or R$ 6,521 for 2021-2022 (depending on 5-hour or 7-hour floor), and Glassdoor indicated an average monthly salary of R$ 3,307 with a range of R$ 2,000 to R$ 7,000. The petitioner's claim appeared to be based on comparing his total monthly remuneration (including bonuses and aids) to the base salaries of other journalists. The record did not contain comparative data showing how his total remuneration compares to that of other journalists, nor did it show his base salary was high compared to other journalists.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Evidence of the petitioner's leadership role within the Brazilian Automotive Press Association.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director concluded, and the AAO confirmed, that the petitioner met this criterion based on evidence of his leadership role within the Brazilian Automotive Press Association. This was the only criterion the Director found satisfied.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner satisfied only one of the required three evidentiary criteria (leading or critical role). Because he could not meet the initial evidentiary requirement of three criteria under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3), the AAO reserved and did not address the remaining contested criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vi) (scholarly articles). The AAO noted that while a final merits determination was not required, it had reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it did not support a finding that the petitioner established the required sustained national or international acclaim, nor that he is among the small percentage at the very top of his field of endeavor. The AAO emphasized that even performers at the major league level do not automatically meet the extraordinary ability standard, and the petitioner's work did not reflect a career of acclaimed work as contemplated by Congress.
legal_citations: Matter of Christo's, Inc., 26 I&N Dec. 537 (AAO 2015)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner waived the issue of lesser nationally or internationally recognized awards under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i) by not contesting the Director's determination on appeal. The AAO reserved the scholarly articles criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vi) as unnecessary to the outcome. No fraud or misrepresentation findings.
